title: Precision Lathe Setup & First-Piece Inspection
description: Learn the complete process for setting up a CNC lathe for a new production run, including tool offsets, workholding, and the critical first-piece inspection that prevents costly batch defects.
intent: Set up a CNC lathe correctly and verify the first piece meets tolerance before running production
sequenceDependencies: Job traveler review (card_001) must come before program loading (card_003)
Tool offsets (card_006) must be set before first cycle (card_004)
First-piece inspection (card_007) must pass before production release (card_010)

cards.title: Review the Job Traveler
cards.body: Before touching the machine, review the job traveler and blueprint. Check the material spec, required tolerances (±0.001" on diameters), and surface finish callouts. Veteran operators always compare to the last run's notes if available.
cards.imageDescription: Operator holding a printed job traveler next to the machine control panel
commonMistakes: Skipping the blueprint and working from memory of a similar job
Not checking if the material spec changed from the last run
cards.watchFor: New hires often glance at the traveler but don't actually read the tolerance callouts

environmentalCues.description: Red-stamped revision marks on updated blueprints
environmentalCues.significance: Indicates dimensions changed since last run — must re-verify all offsets

cards.title: Gather Required Tooling
cards.body: Collect the insert holders, boring bars, and cutting inserts specified on the setup sheet. Check each insert for chips or wear before installing — a chipped insert will scrap your first piece and waste 20 minutes.
cards.imageDescription: Workbench with organized tooling: insert holders, boring bars, and a box of carbide inserts
commonMistakes: Grabbing the wrong insert grade for the material being cut
Not inspecting inserts under good light before installing
cards.watchFor: Check insert edges under direct light — hairline chips are invisible at a glance

environmentalCues.description: Shiny reflection line on a chipped insert edge
environmentalCues.significance: A chipped edge catches light differently — dull spots mean wear, bright lines mean chips

environmentalCues.description: Running a fingernail along the insert edge
environmentalCues.significance: A good edge feels smooth; a chipped edge catches your nail

cards.title: Load the Program & Verify
cards.body: Load the CNC program from the network drive. Before running, use the control's graphic verify mode to simulate the toolpath. Watch for collisions, especially on the Z-axis approach to the chuck jaws.
cards.imageDescription: CNC control screen showing a simulation of the toolpath in green lines
commonMistakes: Loading an old revision of the program
Skipping graphic verify because 'it worked last time'
cards.watchFor: Always verify the program revision number matches the job traveler

environmentalCues.description: Red collision warnings on the graphic verify screen
environmentalCues.significance: Red zones show where the tool would crash into the chuck or workpiece

cards.title: Chuck Jaw Clearance Check
cards.body: CRITICAL: Before first cycle, manually jog each axis to verify clearance between the tool and chuck jaws. A crash here can damage the spindle ($15,000+ repair) and send shrapnel. Use single-block mode on the first run.
cards.imageDescription: Close-up of tool holder positioned near chuck jaws with visible clearance gap
commonMistakes: Trusting graphic verify alone without physical jog check
Running full-speed on the first cycle instead of single-block
cards.watchFor: This is where the most expensive mistakes happen — never rush this step

environmentalCues.description: Slight metallic ping if tool is too close to jaws during jog
environmentalCues.significance: Any contact sound means STOP immediately — collision is imminent

environmentalCues.description: Minimum 1/4 inch visible gap between tool and jaw
environmentalCues.significance: If you can't see daylight between tool and jaw, the clearance is too tight

cards.title: Listen to the Cut
cards.body: Experienced machinists listen to the cutting sound. A smooth, consistent hum means good chip load. A squeal means the feed rate is too low or the insert is dull. A chatter sound means vibration — check tool stickout length.
cards.imageDescription: Operator leaning slightly toward the machine with a focused expression, listening
commonMistakes: Wearing earbuds or not paying attention to machine sounds
Confusing normal coolant pump noise with cutting chatter
cards.watchFor: Sound is the fastest diagnostic tool — learn to distinguish hum, squeal, and chatter

environmentalCues.significance: Good chip load — feed rate and speed are correct
Feed rate too low or insert is dull — tool is rubbing instead of cutting
Tool stickout is too long, causing harmonics — shorten stickout or reduce speed

cards.title: Set Tool Offsets
cards.body: Touch off each tool against the face and diameter of the workpiece. Enter offsets into the tool offset table. Double-check by re-touching — a 0.001" error here becomes a scrap part. Pros always verify offsets twice.
cards.imageDescription: Operator using an edge finder to touch off a tool, with offset table visible on the control screen
commonMistakes: Entering the offset with the wrong sign (positive vs negative)
Forgetting to zero the indicator before touch-off
Only checking offset once instead of double-verifying
cards.watchFor: Offset sign errors are the #1 cause of scrapped first pieces

environmentalCues.description: Slight resistance when edge finder clicks off center
environmentalCues.significance: The moment of contact — this is your reference point

environmentalCues.description: Edge finder wobble stops and runs true
environmentalCues.significance: Tool is exactly on the workpiece edge

cards.title: First-Piece Inspection
cards.body: After the first part is cut, measure ALL critical dimensions with calibrated instruments. Use a micrometer for diameters and a bore gauge for internal features. Document every measurement on the inspection sheet. This catches programming errors before they become 100-piece scrap runs.
cards.imageDescription: Operator measuring a finished part with a digital micrometer, inspection sheet on the bench
commonMistakes: Only measuring one or two dimensions instead of all callouts
Using uncalibrated instruments
Not documenting measurements — relying on memory
cards.watchFor: Measure EVERY callout on the blueprint, not just the ones you think matter

environmentalCues.description: Consistent resistance when closing micrometer on the part
environmentalCues.significance: Proper measuring pressure — too tight gives false readings

cards.title: The Thumbnail Test
cards.body: For surface finish checks, experienced operators drag a thumbnail across the surface. You can feel chatter marks or tool lines that the eye might miss. If your nail catches, the finish is too rough — adjust feed rate or nose radius.
cards.imageDescription: Close-up of an operator's thumb running across a machined surface
commonMistakes: Only doing a visual check without the tactile test
Testing on the wrong surface (non-critical area)
cards.watchFor: This takes practice — start on known good and bad parts to calibrate your feel

environmentalCues.description: Smooth glide across the machined surface
environmentalCues.significance: Surface finish meets spec — no chatter marks
Surface too rough — adjust feed rate or use a larger nose radius insert

cards.title: Hot Chips & Coolant
cards.body: Never reach into the machine while it's running to clear chips. Metal chips are razor-sharp and can be over 500°F. Use an air gun or chip hook after the spindle has stopped. Always wear safety glasses — coolant splash is the #1 eye injury in machine shops.
cards.imageDescription: Blue-hot metal chips curling off the workpiece during a turning operation
commonMistakes: Reaching in with gloves (gloves can get caught in rotating parts)
Using compressed air while the machine is running — blows hot chips into face
cards.watchFor: The urge to clear chips while running is strong — ALWAYS wait for full stop

environmentalCues.description: Blue or straw-colored chips indicate high cutting temperature
environmentalCues.significance: Blue = too hot, silver = correct temperature, straw = borderline

environmentalCues.description: Burning oil smell from overheated coolant
environmentalCues.significance: Coolant breaking down — check flow rate and concentration

cards.title: Document & Release to Production
cards.body: Once the first piece passes inspection, sign off on the setup sheet, save the program revision, and note any offset adjustments for the next operator. A good setup sheet saves the next person an hour of guesswork.
cards.imageDescription: Operator writing on a setup sheet next to the completed first piece
commonMistakes: Not recording offset adjustments made during setup
Forgetting to save the updated program revision
cards.watchFor: Future you (or the next shift) will thank you for detailed notes

quizQuestions.question: Why should you run graphic verify before the first cycle?
options: To check for toolpath collisions that could damage the machine
To make the program run faster
To calibrate the measuring instruments
To warm up the spindle motor
quizQuestions.explanation: Graphic verify simulates the toolpath without moving the machine, letting you catch potential collisions — especially between tools and chuck jaws — before they cause expensive damage.
quizQuestions.commonWrongAnswer: To warm up the spindle motor
quizQuestions.whyStudentsMiss: Students confuse program simulation with machine warm-up procedures, which are separate operations

quizQuestions.question: What does a squealing sound during cutting typically indicate?
options: The coolant pump needs maintenance
The feed rate is too low or the insert is dull
The program is running correctly
The part is finished
quizQuestions.explanation: A squeal means the tool isn't cutting properly — either the feed rate is too low (the tool is rubbing instead of cutting) or the insert edge has worn down. Both reduce surface finish quality and tool life.
quizQuestions.whyStudentsMiss: Students associate unusual machine sounds with equipment malfunction rather than cutting parameters

quizQuestions.question: When is it safe to reach into the machine to clear chips?
options: When wearing leather gloves
During a pause in the program
Only after the spindle has fully stopped
When the coolant is turned off
quizQuestions.explanation: Metal chips can be razor-sharp and extremely hot (500°F+). Never reach in while any part is moving. Wait for the spindle to fully stop and use a chip hook or air gun. Gloves can actually get caught in rotating parts.
quizQuestions.whyStudentsMiss: Students assume PPE makes any action safe, but gloves near rotating machinery create entanglement risk

quizQuestions.question: Why do experienced machinists check tool offsets twice?
options: Company policy requires it
A 0.001" error becomes a scrapped part
The machine resets offsets automatically
To wear in the new tool
quizQuestions.explanation: Tool offset errors translate directly to dimensional errors on the part. Even a thousandth of an inch (0.001") can push a feature out of tolerance. Double-checking is faster than scrapping parts and re-setting up.
quizQuestions.whyStudentsMiss: Students default to 'because rules' rather than understanding the engineering reason behind the practice

quizQuestions.question: What does a blue chip color indicate during turning?
options: Correct cutting parameters
The material is too hard
Excessive cutting temperature
The coolant is working properly
quizQuestions.explanation: Blue chips indicate the cutting temperature is too high. Silver spirals are ideal. Adjust speed, feed, or coolant flow to reduce heat generation.
quizQuestions.whyStudentsMiss: Blue steel looks 'cool' and professional to new hires who haven't learned heat tempering colors

quizQuestions.question: What should you do if your thumbnail catches on a machined surface?
options: The part is acceptable — file it smooth
Adjust feed rate or use a larger nose radius insert
Apply more coolant and re-run the program
The surface finish is within spec
quizQuestions.explanation: A catching thumbnail means the surface finish is too rough — chatter marks or tool lines are present. Adjusting the feed rate or switching to a larger nose radius insert will improve the finish.
quizQuestions.commonWrongAnswer: The part is acceptable — file it smooth
quizQuestions.whyStudentsMiss: New hires think post-processing fixes are acceptable, but filing changes dimensions and isn't a proper solution

tacitCues.description: The operator glances at the chip color and curl pattern to judge cutting temperature — blue chips mean too much heat, silver spirals are ideal
Before tightening the chuck, the operator taps the workpiece with a brass hammer to seat it flat — prevents runout that instruments might not catch until after the cut
The operator checks coolant flow direction by watching the first few seconds of the cut — adjusts the nozzle so coolant hits the cutting edge, not just the part
After measuring, the operator wipes the micrometer anvils with a lint-free cloth — oil from fingers causes 0.0002" measurement errors

tools.name: Digital Micrometer (0-1")
tools.safetyNotes: Handle with clean hands, store in case when not in use

tools.name: Bore Gauge
tools.purpose: Measuring internal diameters and verifying bore dimensions
tools.safetyNotes: Calibrate against ring gauge before each use

tools.name: Edge Finder
tools.purpose: Precisely locating the workpiece edge for setting tool offsets
tools.safetyNotes: Run at low RPM only, remove before starting the program

tools.name: Chip Hook
tools.purpose: Safely removing metal chips from the work area
tools.safetyNotes: Never use hands to clear chips, even with gloves

tools.name: Brass Hammer
tools.purpose: Seating workpiece flat against chuck jaws without marring the surface
tools.safetyNotes: Light taps only — excessive force can damage the chuck

safetyHazards.description: Spindle collision with chuck jaws during first cycle of a new program
safetyHazards.mitigation: Always run graphic verify and use single-block mode on the first cycle. Manually jog to check clearance.

safetyHazards.description: Hot metal chips causing burns or eye injury
safetyHazards.mitigation: Wear safety glasses at all times. Never reach into the machine while the spindle is running. Use chip hooks and air guns.
No loose clothing, jewelry, or gloves near the spindle. Keep hands clear of the chuck during operation.
Keep machine enclosure doors closed during cutting. Ensure mist collection system is functioning.

sequenceChallenges.title: Lathe Setup Sequence
sequenceChallenges.instructions: Drag these setup steps into the correct order
steps: Gather required tooling and inspect inserts
Load CNC program and run graphic verify
Check chuck jaw clearance by manual jog
Run first piece in single-block mode

sequenceChallenges.title: First-Piece Inspection Flow
sequenceChallenges.instructions: Put these inspection steps in the right order
steps: Measure all critical dimensions with calibrated instruments
Document every measurement on inspection sheet
Sign off and release to production

spotDetailChallenges.question: What must you check before tightening the chuck on a new workpiece?
spotDetailChallenges.hint: Think about what the veteran does with a brass hammer...

spotDetailChallenges.question: What are the three cutting sounds you should learn to distinguish?
spotDetailChallenges.hint: One is good, one means dull/slow feed, one means vibration...

spotDetailChallenges.question: Why should you never wear gloves near the spindle?
spotDetailChallenges.hint: Think about what happens to loose material near rotating parts...

flashChallenges.question: How hot can metal chips get during turning?
What's the typical tolerance for precision lathe diameters?
What color are chips when cutting temperature is too high?
How much can a spindle crash repair cost?

simulationChallenges.title: Match the Tool to Its Purpose
simulationChallenges.instructions: Connect each tool to the task it performs during lathe setup

simulationChallenges.title: Chip Color Diagnosis
simulationChallenges.instructions: Match each chip color to what it tells you about the cut

interactiveModels.title: CNC Lathe Chuck Assembly
interactiveModels.description: Inspect the chuck assembly — tap each hotspot to learn what each component does and why it matters for setup

hotspots.label: Jaw #1
hotspots.description: Three jaws clamp the workpiece concentrically. Must be clean — a single chip under a jaw causes runout and scrapped parts.

hotspots.label: Jaw #2
hotspots.description: All jaws move together via a scroll plate. Tighten evenly — over-torquing one side distorts the workpiece.

hotspots.label: Jaw #3
hotspots.description: Check jaw wear marks periodically. Worn jaws lose grip and can eject the workpiece at high RPM.

hotspots.label: Center Bore
hotspots.description: The through-hole for bar stock. Diameter limits the max stock size. Never run stock that barely fits — it'll jam.

hotspots.label: Scroll Plate
hotspots.description: The internal mechanism that moves all jaws simultaneously. Listen for grinding — it means chips got inside.

transcript: Alright, so first thing we're gonna do is review the job traveler. You always want to check your tolerances before you start touching the machine... [continues with full process narration including tool offset setup, first piece inspection, and production release]
